comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of George Washington Carey, another American occultist too rarely remembered today. One of the major figures in the end of homeopathy that uses the twelve biochemic cell salts, he worked out a detailed system of spiritual and physical healing using those twelve remedies, assigning them to the signs of the Zodiac. For much of the twentieth century, most of the correspondence courses offered by occult schools included lessons on the cell salts lifted with or without credit from Carey's work, and the kind of old-fashioned health food store that catered to alternative spirituality as well as alternative diets inevitably had all twelve cell salts in stock. (Longtime Seattle residents may recall the charming Mari-Don Healthways store on 45th street in Wallingford; that's where I got my first cell salts, back when I was first learning to use them.) The wheel below on the right, which can be found in various places online, is one of Carey's creations. 

[IMAGE:1]Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***
[IMAGE:2]

comments.content: Going to r/occult and, indeed, many other occult themed forums, it appears that the plurality and perhaps majority of topics are on evil magic and demonolotary. Most religious institutions and professionals -- at least in affluent regions of the United States -- behave as if the gods they ostensibly worship are mere superstition and nonsense. My questions are: 1) how long do you expect the prevailing attitudes towards the spiritual realms to remain this toxic and 2) what do you think will it take to shake folks out of this torpor?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) This is normally what happens when popular culture is about to take a sharp turn away from magic. People start by embracing the mainstream religious attitude toward magic -- "it's all devil worship" -- and end by embracing the rest of mainstream religion, and becoming Methodists or what have you. I expect a lot of the people who are currently prattling about summoning demons, within a few years, will be testifying in church about how Jesus saved them from evil -- the way that a lot of ex-hippies did in the early 1970s, to cite the most recent example. It's a recurring pattern.

2) It's self-terminating, as noted above. Then we'll have around 36 years when occultism is off the radar screens of contemporary culture and most of the people who are involved are serious about it, followed by another round of pop-culture occultism.

That's why it's absolutely essential just now for ethical occultists to distance themselves from pop-culture occultism and especially from its current plunge into malevolent magic, and to make methods of magical protection that are compatible with mainstream religions readily available to everyone. People freak out about devil worship when they're afraid of being targeted by noxious magic and have no defenses.

Oddly enough, I'll be talking more about this on Wednesday...

replies.title: Re: Will it get dangerous for occultists?

Thank you for this! I can only agree fervently. The thing too is that such simple protective measures can make such a big difference; light a bay leaf on fire over a bowl of water and drop it in and say out loud, "I pray that this water purifies all pollution.'" sprinkle it about and see the miracle. Post images of hexafoils about a space and see the miracle. Carry a piece of lapis lazuli or agate and see the miracle. Wash a space with lemongrass essential oil in vinegar and see the miracle.

The issue though is a very delicate one to navigate; since many people are caught in a double bind where they don't believe in magic and the supernatural, but they do believe in unseen, organized evil. Indeed, I credit this double bind with much of the demonolotary since people sincerely do not believe benevolent beings but do believe in evil while simultaneously not believing in anything besides the material. I think that the work you do is excellent for mitigating these problems, but the dynamic, shared widely and with religious professionals as well, seems difficult to resolve since the double bind can prevent effective countermeasures to said organized evil

comments.content:
Hi JMG!

You mentioned that you are not at all a fan of Michael Harner's Shamanism.

What is wrong about it, and could it be salvaged by implementing changes in the practice?

What are the differences between shamanistic journeys and scrying in the spirit vision?

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Harner's system is basically a simplified form of scrying in the spirit vision, without a coherent symbolic system to give it meaning or anything else to keep it from descending into what Israel Regardie used to call "astral tourism." It's entertaining, no doubt, but that's all it is.

Real shamanism -- and I encourage you to read actual accounts of trad shamanic practice to get a sense of the differences -- is not something you choose, not something you control, and not something you imagine. The spirits choose you, they take you away to their realm, and you don't just imagine the flesh being ripped from your bones and your organs replaced by pieces of crystal -- you experience it. Meanwhile your family keeps watch over your comatose body and wonders whether you'll survive, because sometimes people don't. If you come back, you live forever after in both worlds at once, and that means that you face serious dangers other people don't, because the world of the spirits isn't just inhabited by friendly animal helpers, it has things in it that can and will kill you.

That's real shamanism. Do you see the differences?

Some of the Other Places may indeed be safe to visit, but I would not care to go exploring in general without some sort of very experienced guide, who moreover would probably not be a human at all.  --  And yes, in principle, a predator from one of the Other Places might look for prey here, if it was even aware of Our Place among the vast multitude of Places.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's very nearly a classic shamanic cosmology. Most shamanic traditions divide the world of spirits into some number (usually 7 or 9) upper worlds and as many underworlds, and many of them are typically stocked with critters that think humans and/or their souls are flavorful and crunchy. The shaman can go there because he has one or more guardian spirits, who in fact are almost always nonhuman, but even so it's a risky business.

Among some of the Puget Sound native peoples, a group of shamans will get together and basically raid the Otherworld, traveling there via a magical canoe. It's a community event -- you have a longhouse in perfect darkness dozens of drummers pounding intricate rhythms with drumsticks on a long wooden board, the shamans sitting in a line as they would in one of the big Salish dugout canoes, and for hours they paddle their way to the spirit world, get whatever they came for (for example, the soul of a sick person), and bring it back with them. It's a dangerous journey, and the spirits always fight to hold onto whatever they've taken. Reading accounts of the ceremony always reminds me of Taliesin's poem Preiddeu Annwn:

"Three freights of Prydwen were they that went with Arthur;
Save seven, none returned from Caer Sidi."

FWIW, As a lay Catholic you have a lot more resources available to you than you might realize.

First, the kinds of natural magics discussed here-- the use of vinegar, say, or a cut onion, or protective herbs to repel spirits is very much within the bounds of the Catholic tradition. St. Hildegard of Bingen, a doctor of the Church, recommends several various natural substances for spiritual protection in her Physica, and St. Raphael the Archangel does the same in the Book of Tobit. If they can do it, why not you?

Second, as a baptized Christian, you have the authority to bless your own home. Blessings are sacramentals, which draw their power from the operator, and you officially have the right to bless anything under your authority. Get some holy water and blessed incense-- you can bless these yourself, too, if necessary. You can follow the traditional formula for a home blessing, or you can simply go through your home room by room, sprinkling the holy water while praying the Asperges Me and censing with a suitable prayer. Or both.

Finally I second JMG's recommendation of St. Michael-- he's the real deal, and you can probably find candles dedicated to him at your local grocery store. And remember that your home has its own guardian angel as well.

comments.title: Locality in Astrology
comments.content:
I’ve been gradually working through my natal chart, however, I’m a little unclear regarding just how much the ascendant and house changes from current location should color horoscope interpretation. Is it something you ignore entirely? Or do you need to filter everything in your chart through the place you lived at any given time? For instance, in my natal chart, I’m a rising cancer with Venus in Accidental Detriment in the first house (it’s in Leo, so it takes its dignity from Aries which rules the first house right?), Mercury and Mars (in accidental detriment) in the second house, the Sun in the third house, Saturn and Uranus (both in accidental detriment) in the fifth house, Neptune (in accidental detriment) in the sixth house, and nothing within 12 degrees of any of my angles. Where I live now, my ascendant has moved to Leo, my first house is occupied by Mercury and a Mars that’s in accidental dignity, the Sun’s moved to the second house, Saturn’s moved to the fourth house (still in accidental detriment), Neptune’s moved to the fifth house, the moon’s moved to the ninth house, and Venus has moved to a place of accidental exaltation in the twelfth house (which move the Moon, Mars, Neptune, and Venus out of detriment). Meanwhile, Venus is within one degree from my ascendant, and Jupiter is one degree from my descendent. Do I need to look at each of those (and the other two charts for the two places I’ve lived between now and then) as completely separate horoscopes for each of those periods of my life? Or do the changes affect my natal chart in more subtle ways (for instance, when looking at my ruling planet placement, would that be the tenth house as in my natal chart, the 9th house based on the moon’s movement when I relocated, a combination of the two, or the second house since my ruling planet is now the sun)? In Llewellyn George’s chapter on relocation, he starts out by saying that the Natal Chart is the Natal Chart no matter what, but then in his example, he lists some pretty significant changes that come from the shifts in house placement (for instance, a move of planets from the fourth to fifth house changing interests in the areas of life ruled by those planets from family to recreation), which would imply that locality does affect the horoscope in ways that need to be taken into account but he doesn’t go into much more detail than that in terms of how to balance nativity and locality when putting together a horoscope.

-Eric Singletary

replies.title: Re: Locality in Astrology
replies.content:
Okay, your birth chart is your birth chart and remains the same wherever you go. The changes you get from locality are overlaid on the basic pattern of the birth chart. I'd encourage you to cast relocation charts for each place you've lived, and then look back over what was different about your life in each of those places; that'll give you a good sense of how this works.

Reading astrological charts is a knack, and the only way to develop it is by looking at lots of astrological charts!

replies.title: Re: Locality in Astrology
replies.content:
Thank you for this. The number of moving parts to keep track of with this is mind-boggling and more than a little overwhelming with signs, dignities, houses, aspects, angles, and so on, each coloring the other in intricate ways. Right now what I’ve been doing as I delineate is working in a four columned chart with each location but focusing on my natal chart while occasionally glancing at my location to see if it says anything interesting.

So... in answer to my ascendant/moon question looking at what I’ve written out so far: my basic nature is hypersensitive, shy and retiring (not helped by both Mercury and Mars being squared with Saturn making communication and self confidence my primary limitations), my moon as the ruling planet in the tenth house means I tend towards chasing after a dream of an idea life that doesn’t really exist so I get lost in fantasies more than actually pursuing my dreams. Once I move, my sign shifts to Leo, I start being put in unasked for positions of authority at work, in my religious communities, and in social organizations, Mars and Mercury move to the first house which brings the Saturn-squared Mars and Mercury from a place of limiting my material success in the world to a place where overcoming them becomes core to my identity (which combined with the Leo shift makes the pursuit of self confidence, communication skills and active “solar” qualities a core theme of this half of my life, but that balances rather than supplants my basic nature meaning those qualities will never come naturally to me). The Sun’s move to the second house now no longer occupied by afflicted planets opens up chances for finding stable employment, and the Moon’s move to the ninth house shifts my ambitions from an idealized concept of the perfect vocation to spiritual attainment which makes my happiness less contingent on finding the perfect career but that shift remains rooted in tenth house energies leading me to treat my spirituality with all the perfectionism and ambition of a career and leading to a danger of viewing spiritual development in terms of achievement, obsessing over successes and failures. Does that extremely amateur stab sound like I’m roughly on the right track with interpreting the effects of locality?

comments.title: Magic affecting the material world
comments.content:
JMG

I’ve seen you post the Dion Fortune definition of magic many times and also explain (in your Monsters book I think?) that magic is not meant to affect the physical/material realm and you need science and engineering for that.

I also seem to vaguely recall that you said somewhere else that magic can have material effects in some rare and highly specific circumstances.

Can magic ever have a direct physical or
Material effect?

replies.title: Re: Magic affecting the material world
replies.content: Good question. The most straightforward answer is "we just don't know." So little of the old strong lore survives intact that we have only the most sketchy ideas about what is possible at the upper end of magical practice. I will say that in more than forty years of magical practice I've never seen anyone cause a direct physical or material effect, and I've never managed the trick myself.

replies.title: Re: Magic affecting the material world
replies.content:
I think weather working, especially on a small scale, may be an exception. I've been present a couple of times when witches persuaded winds and clouds to shift a little so that gathering rain clouds parted just long enough to perform a ritual. The moment the rite ended, down came the rain. Nobody made a big deal of doing this.

The solar eclipse that was the occasion for the jacket photograph on the hardcover first edition of Drawing Down the Moon was reported to me as another instance of this. A bunch of neopagans and other magical practitioners gathered at the Stonehenge replica somewhere in the US (Oregon?) for a solar eclipse. There was cloud cover over the site and it was socked in for miles around. According to the story, before totality the sky cleared over the site, and only over the site, and they got a good view.

Another report from the same era (mid Sixties-Seventies) involved a witch I know and respect, doing an outdoor ritual with other people I know, but I didn't happen to be there on the occasion when (so I've been told) she decided to invoke the quarter guardians by calling a breeze to spring up from the east, the south, the west, and then the north, and they each obliged her.

I have two possible explanations which are not mutually exclusive. One is that you manage this kind of effect by asking an air spirit to do it for you. The other is that altering the material world by psychokinesis is easier if what you want to change is within view and is of a fluid, light, changeable nature. It's easier to tip over something that is delicately balanced than something that is already at rest.

replies.title: Re: Magic affecting the material world
replies.content: Yes, in principle you can get a physical effect if you can work through a human mind and body, whether it's your own body or another's.  If you work to affect the mind that controls the body, then the affected body may be able to produce physical effects by the same sort of actions that it uses to get anything else done in the material, mundane world.

Hi Anonymous,

Re: disturbing and fascinating hypnogogia

I have experienced this kind of thing since I was very small. It was one of the reasons I begged to be allowed to sleep in my mom's bed long after I should have been sleeping the whole night in my own. The solution that worked for young-child me was to turn a radio on at low but audible volume on the classical music station. I could focus on the music and drift off to solid sleep in my own bed the whole night with little disturbing interference. I wasn't so concerned with the delightful imagery then.

As adult, magic-practicing me, now I make use of my color breathing. I draw in brightness from above with a tinge of whatever best counters the "vibe" of the disturbance, then exhale it into a protective sphere around I and my partner, pushing out from my body to roughly the room-space. All this while not getting up, and taking care to physically move as little as possible. That usually makes the good images as well as the bad dissolve, but the good tend to resurface again and the bad don't.

Bonnie

I've been practicing about five minutes of relaxation and color breathing as part of my (close to) daily routine for nearly 2 years now. The first year it was whatever color appealed to me at the time, and this year it's been whatever color corresponds to the previous of the eight Druid holidays as given in The Druidry Handbook, making a round of the spectrum through the passing of one year.

I will usually do three cycles of breath with white light or "brightness", then nine cycles of color, then three more cycles of white or brightness to finish. I use the fourfold breath, and practice relaxing my body along with the breathing.

It didn't take long to be useful though- I started using it occasionally in daily life when things got tense or unpleasant nearly right away. Every now and again I use it at night when the room gets that weird feeling just before I start seeing/sensing things. Definitely a "something's coming" feel. It does happen very fast!

One thing I noticed is that regular practicing of relaxing is just as helpful as the imagining the energy/color movement. Relaxation improves control. Learning to relax when you are not frightened or feeling threatened makes it more likely that you will be able to relax when you -are- feeling frightened or threatened. For me, the more I relax my physical body, the stronger the flow and intensity of the light is. YMMV

I haven't used pentagrams during bouts of hypnogogia. I think because it's more visceral and faster for me to breathe in and concentrate the light then push it outwards from me in a protective sphere. I've used pentagrams while awake, but not in the in-between.

Especially if I'm in a borderline conscious state! Otherwise, I'm fine with discouraging/ending intrusions by whatever method is effective and appropriate. Bonnie

You can find the old ritual at the link below: some revised Mass priests (including ours) still use it, but usually you have to find a church that celebrates the Latin mass to have this ritual used.
https://www.sanctamissa.org/en/resources/books-1962/rituale-romanum/48-blessings-for-special-days-and-feasts.html

I find the impact of the Rosary beneficial however I say it, but if I focus on the mysteries (I have little symbols of them in my head to look at) it makes me more clear-headed and able to exercise self-control, while if I focus on the prayers it makes me more aware of God's presence in the everyday world.
SamChevre

replies.title: Re: so many questions
replies.content:
To your third question, look up information on the water spirits that the Russians (and some other Slavs) call "rusalki." They are female, and they are considered to be quite dangerous to the living, especially men. Probably most of them used to be women who had been made pregnant, then abandoned by the father of their baby to fend for themselves, and kicked out of their family and community for an out-of-wedlock pregnancy. So their animosity toward the living, and especially toward men, makes a lot of sense.

Russian peasant villages are traditionally by a river or stream or lake, and Russian peasant women traditionally did not learn to swim, so there was always an easy means at hand for such a woman to take her own life. But it's not a pleasant death, and spirits of the untimely dead (and in particular the violently dead) have been considered to be very dangerous to the living as far back as Antiquity.

comments.title: Astrological Timing for Beginners
comments.content:
Hello,

I have a question about how beginners can track astrological timing. There was a conversation on one of the MM where perhaps you and Tripp (?) were comparing notes on how you keep a chalkboard or list of moon phase, signs, transits, etc. I’m a visual person so a daily chart makes a lot of sense to me.

I also would like to start working with Astrology (I have my birthchart and the old George A-Z book) but am not sure what Step 1 would be to move that from a report and analysis to something I can use in daily life.

Questions:
1- Do you know of a print version (an ephemeris?) that I can purchase so I don’t have to go online everyday. I’ve looked at the big book website with the jungle name to see but I don’t know if those are ones you would recommend.

Moon phase and sign. Transits (which ones)? Something else? A gardener might have a different template than someone looking at astrology for daily use but maybe not?

3 – Should I keep a journal of this? It seems like a lot of data to track and I’m not sure where to start with it.

Thank you very much for MM.

AL

PS – I did all my seed starts, transplants, plantings in Cancer, Scorpio, Pisces or Taurus this season. My garden has marginal soil which I have been improving for 3 years but the difference between this year and last with how easily plants take off has been noticeable…certainly, there’s a little more compost and we’ve had a little more rain but the plants are doing disproportionately better. replies.title: Re: Astrological Timing for Beginners
replies.content:
1) Yes, and in fact that's what I use. The Rosicrucian Ephemeris is the one I use, and I order it annually from the American Federation of Astrologers online bookstore.

2) It's best to get into it a step at a time. Start by tracking the Moon through her phases and signs. Then pay attention to which house of your natal chart the Moon is in -- Llewellyn George devotes several pages to this. Then start paying attention when any of the planets makes a major aspect (conjunction, sextile, square, trine, or opposition) to one of your natal planets. Then start tracking the aspects the Moon makes -- it'll go through around a sign each day, so there tend to be a lot of those.

3) I use a spiral notebook for the purpose. It's a lot easier when you write it all down.

comments.title: Mental health and magic
comments.content:
My question is about what kind or degree of mental health issues should stop one from practicing ceremonial magic. Naturally, I'll use myself as a handy example. I've had bouts of depression most of my life, sometimes including suicidal thoughts but never actions. (And I've tried therapy several times, with limited success.) I have probably garden-variety anxiety and social issues. I've never had what I regard as worse symptoms, such as delusions, hearing voices, or detachment from reality. Also, no addiction issues.
I've been practicing the SOP, and if anything I think it's benefiting me, but I wonder if more advanced practices would also be safe. What kind of issues should rule out magical practice? Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Mental health and magic
replies.content: As I understand it, people with schizophrenia or anything related to it should stay far away from magical practice, as they tend to worsen drastically. Depression seems to be more variable -- some people with depression find that magical practice helps, while others get no benefit or find their symptoms worsening. I'd encourage you to be careful, and to get at least one other person to help you keep track of your mental stability, but if it seems to be helping, go ahead.

replies.title: Re: Mental health and magic
replies.content: Data point: so far, at least, it's helped considerably with my anxiety. In addition to the explicitly magical elements, the practice of fourfold breath and then discursive meditation is really good for breaking the circling thoughts that are my main symptom.

replies.title: Re: Mental health and magic
replies.content:
Please note there's Neurotic Depression and there's Psycotic Depression. While you will probably need a licenced Psycoanalyst to diagnose your case, the layman's way to tell is if your I-you relationships are holding or not: if you are able to function and sustain relationships with the people closer to you, even if your mood is making those social dynamics somewhat disfunctional, your are probably on the Neurotic side of the fence. If you are withdrawing from human contact, it is a warning sign that something deeper may be cooking up.

Now that I think about it. If you think that your mental health is brittle and don't want to risk trying ceremonial magic, you may want to try some of the arts that make use of your etheric body: performance arts (music, dance; though acting do have an ill fame), external martial arts, non esoteric yoga, etc. Basically, seek deliverate practices that have a tradition behind it, any sport will not cut it. This kind of lesser energy work will ground you into balance, either enough to practice magic later in this life, of to lay the foundation to do it later on.

comments.title: Meditations on automation, modernity
comments.content:
Hello,
For some reason I have come to be very concerned with automation in general.
Here in Ecnarf it looks as if the Radiance had somehow won forever.
Automatic cashiers in supermarkets, bank offices closing down, the tech sector also automating tertiary jobs now (with chatbots, website based companies taking care of secretary jobs, of food delivery...). A glorious future where people use electric cars, city centers devoid of cars (and of non-hipster people I guess, since small towns do their best to develop car-centric shopping malls).
The problem is that this glorious future is not making life better for quite a few people who are dear to me, and I hate it for aesthetic reasons as well. I have been used to antique-looking surroundings and I like to live in places that stimulate the imagination rather than in a giant set of glass cubes.

I am myself tied between two worlds, however, with somewhat rural roots and on the other hand depending on overly qualified jobs to make a (somewhat okay) living.

I know it will soon be time to make a transition, I try my best know to learn practical stuff instead of wallowing in modern media like video games... Yet I feel I will be stuck in this lifestyle, thinking about what it would be like to work with one's hands or with living beings (plants, animals...)

Having read what people say here about meditation, it seems to be a good tool. I feel that I should also work on myself instead of worrying about the outside world, in order to respond more effectively and reach a better harmony with my inclinations.

It's hard to explain like that, but what hints or meditation exercises would you recommend to tackle these big existential questions? In order to bring myself to more constructive demeanour.

Thanks as always!

Puzzled

replies.title: Re: Meditations on automation, modernity
replies.content: Well, the first thing to do is get some skill at meditation. Tackling the big existential questions is like mountaineering in the Alps -- you want to get some experience on smaller and less challenging mountains first!

comments.title: Mindfulness
comments.content:
Today I've another one... concerning "mindfulness". I stumbled over the topic because a colleague handed a magazine published by some German mindfulness association to me. Apparently, there is a strong movement to establish these practices in preschool, school and university. A key factor, which is named in many articles and interviews, is the training of teachers. In some federal states in Germany, training programs are already available for free for school teachers. The long term plan is to make this stuff integral part in the training of teachers as well as in the education of children.

I recently re-read your Kek-wars posts for some other reason and then the magazine came around and now some alarm bells are ringing. To sum up the mantra of "mindfulness" as I understand it... take some buddhist practices, strip them of their spiritual and technical context (for example, it's explicitly stripped from the practice of concentration), do some studies that show the positive effects on the brain (for whatever that means and however you "show" them...) and then push it to the masses. Do I want something more than to teach the kids how to function as another gear in this mad world? Yes, certainly. Do I want it to be this stuff? I'm far from sure...

The whole thing saddens me. I don't know about the situation in the USA, but here you always need some fancy label for the things we are teaching our children and for the way how we do it. Be it "quality management", "competence orientated education" (wow!), "performance orientation" or now "mindfulness". It seems to be impossible to start with the very simple, efficient things like a school building that does not have the look and feel of a ruin or a jail (or both) and good food for those who have to stay for the afternoon. Instead the children can eat junk inside a ruin but hey - after lunch there's mindfulness class...

You've said some words on "mindfulness" in your kek-articles - however I'd be very grateful for your take on that matter, especially in this educational context.

Nachtgurke

I have been experimenting with the hoodoo bath and floor wash to very good effect. It has brought up the following question about salt.

There is a fabulous Korean spa I used to go to, where there are several infrared sauna rooms. This same spa also had a hot room with sand on the floor, and rooms with either charcoal, or a special jade stone embedded in the walls to generate a particular wavelength of heat. I don’t want to empty and refill them every time I use them. Also there is a 25 lb bag of salt in the cupboard waiting to be used (I make salt bags for others). comments.title: Chic taberos self initiation into the golden dawn
comments.content:
Dear jmg
In “self-initiation into the golden dawn” by chic tabero it says that for the zelator grade one must meditate on crystals, it doesn’t give any concise instructions so I was wondering how you would meditate upon crystals?

replies.title: Re: Chic taberos self initiation into the golden dawn
replies.content: How I would do it? I'd do the usual preliminaries for meditation -- sit in a balanced posture, relax my body, breathe rhythmically for a while -- and then call up the concept of a crystal. I'd think about what the word "crystal" means, what crystals are and how they relate to geology and physics; the whole time I'd keep my mind on the subject and bring it back right away if it tried to drift off to some other subject. Then I'd find a train of thought relating to crystals that interested me, and follow it all the way to its end. Then I'd finish the meditation. The next day I'd repeat the exercise, choosing a different train of thought, and I'd keep at it daily until I'd explored the concept thoroughly.
